claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05); Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is 16 and in approved education
Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, please tell me all their ages and I can assess each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I've got two children I'd like to ask about - Alex, who's 6 years old, and Blake, who's 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! Thanks for providing that information. It's great that you're checking eligibility for both children at once. Let me assess each of them.

**Alex (6 years old)** - Since Alex is under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement. I'll continue checking the other criteria for Alex.

**Blake (16 years old)** - Since Blake is 16-19, I'll need to check if they meet the education or training requirements.

Let me navigate through the decision tree for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I have the information I need to continue assessing both children. Let me ask some questions:

---

**For Alex (6 years old):**

Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?

*(This is a standard question for all children - for a 6-year-old, the answer would typically be "yes" as they wouldn't be in employment.)*

---

**For Blake (16 years old):**

Which of the following best describes Blake's current situation?

1. **In education or training** - Blake is currently enrolled in approved education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQs up to level 3, or similar)
2. **Recently left education** - Blake has recently left education or training (within the last 20 weeks)
3. **Neither** - Blake is not in education/training and hasn't recently left

---

Please let me know the answers for both children and we can continue the assessment!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh right, yes - Alex is only 6, so no, he's not working at all!

As for Blake, she's currently in education - she's doing her A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Let me continue the assessment for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me continue Alex's assessment separately:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! Here's where we are:

---

**For Alex (6 years old):** ✅ Great - Alex isn't working, so that's fine. Moving on to the next check.

---

**For Blake (16 years old):** A-levels are on the approved list of qualifications! Just a couple of quick checks about Blake's education:

1. **Is Blake studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)
2. **Is the course funded by an employer or linked to a guaranteed job with an employer?**

I'll also need to ask about Blake's work situation:

3. **Is Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not working at all)?**

---

Once you confirm these details, we can continue to the remaining checks for both children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, Blake's doing full-time A-levels - it's definitely more than 12 hours a week of proper classes and supervised study. No, it's not funded by an employer or anything like that - it's just normal sixth form college.

And no, she's not working at all - she's just focusing on her studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great news for Blake! Her A-levels meet all the education requirements. Let me continue the assessment for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Both children are progressing well through the checks. Now I need to ask about benefits:

---

**For both Alex and Blake:**

Are either of your children receiving any benefits in their own right, such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

*(This would be benefits claimed by the child themselves, not benefits you receive for them.)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are receiving any benefits in their own right. They're just children - they don't claim anything themselves.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's what I expected - neither of them claiming benefits themselves. Now one more question about the children:

---

**For both Alex and Blake:**

Is either of them currently in an apprenticeship? 

*(This is particularly relevant for Blake - if she's doing A-levels at sixth form college, I assume she's not in an apprenticeship, but just want to confirm.)*

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are doing apprenticeships. Alex is only 6, so definitely not! And Blake is just doing A-levels at college - no apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Both children have passed all the child-related checks. Now I need to ask some questions about you (the claimant).

---

**Residency Requirements:**

Do you meet any of the following residency criteria?

1. **You normally live in the UK** - This is the most common situation
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and are working/seeking work/studying with sufficient resources
4. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad (e.g., diplomatic service, armed forces)

For most people, the answer is simply "I normally live in the UK." Does this apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, no - I don't live in the UK. I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Living abroad can still allow eligibility for Child Benefit in certain circumstances.

Could you tell me a bit more about your situation? Specifically:

**Are you living abroad as a Crown servant?**

This would include:
- Working for the UK government overseas (e.g., diplomatic service, Foreign Office)
- Serving in the armed forces stationed abroad
- Certain other government roles abroad

Or is there another reason you're living abroad? For example:
- Temporarily working abroad
- Retired abroad
- Living abroad for personal reasons

This will help me determine if you might still qualify for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or anything like that. I'm just living abroad for personal reasons - not working for the government or in the armed forces.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Based on what you've told me, I'm afraid this affects your eligibility for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to share this outcome with you.

---

## Assessment Result: NOT ELIGIBLE

**Unfortunately, you are not currently eligible to claim Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake.**

---

### Summary for Each Child:

| Child | Age | Child Criteria Met? | Eligible? |
|-------|-----|---------------------|-----------|
| **Alex** | 6 | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Blake** | 16 | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

---

### Why You're Not Eligible:

Both of your children fully meet all the requirements on their side:
- **Alex** qualifies as he's under 16
- **Blake** qualifies as she's in approved full-time education (A-levels)

However, **you (the claimant) do not meet the UK residency requirements**. To claim Child Benefit, you must:
- Normally live in the UK, **OR**
- Have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, **OR**
- Have pre-settled status and be working/studying/have sufficient resources, **OR**
- Be living abroad as a Crown servant (government work, armed forces, etc.)

Since you're living abroad for personal reasons and don't fall into any of these categories, you cannot claim Child Benefit at this time.

---

### What You Could Do:

1. **If you return to live in the UK** - You would likely become eligible to claim for both children, as they both meet the child-related criteria.

2. **If another person in the UK is responsible for the children** - They may be able to claim Child Benefit instead (e.g., if the children live with another family member in the UK).

3. **Check if your country has a social security agreement with the UK** - Some countries have reciprocal arrangements. You could contact HMRC's Child Benefit Office for more information.

---

I'm sorry this wasn't the outcome you were hoping for. Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about the eligibility rules?

reasonings: Alex (6 years old) meets all the child-related eligibility criteria: he is under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. However, the claimant (parent) does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and is not a Crown servant, does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and does not normally live in the UK. Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Alex.

Blake (16 years old) meets all the child-related eligibility criteria: she is in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college, which is 12+ hours per week of supervised study), not employer-funded, not working, not receiving benefits in her own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant (parent) does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and is not a Crown servant, does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and does not normally live in the UK. Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: Both children (Alex and Blake) fully meet all the child-related eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. However, the claimant does not satisfy the residency requirements. To claim Child Benefit, the claimant must normally live in the UK, have settled or pre-settled status (with qualifying criteria), or be a Crown servant living abroad. Since the claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and does not fall into any of these categories, they are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for either child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

However, the claimant (parent) does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and is not a Crown servant, does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and does not normally live in the UK. Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Alex.

Blake (16 years old) meets all the child-related eligibility criteria: she is in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college, which is 12+ hours per week of supervised study), not employer-funded, not working, not receiving benefits in her own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship.
